coredata.dc:description: This study integrated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (nitrogen-doped CDs) with remarkable fluorescence into a high-porosity inverse opal photonic crystal (IOPC) structure. A portable fluorescent hydrogel strip was developed by incorporating molecular imprinted biomimetic recognition, enabling the rapid identification and accurate detection of the insecticide imidacloprid (IMI). The ordered and hierarchical architecture of the IOPCs was advantageous to the uniform dispersion of nitrogen-doped CDs while providing efficient mass transfer channels for IMI. Additionally, the sensing strips achieved adsorption equilibrium within 20 min and demonstrated excellent selectivity, stability, and reusability. They showed a linear response to IMI across the range of 0.1–50 μg/mL (R 2 = 0.9905) with a detection limit of 0.065 μg/mL (S/N = 3). The spiked recoveries ranged from 88.2 % to 102.8 %, aligning well with HPLC results. This indicates that the developed fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing strip is an effective analytical tool for detecting IMI residues in food products.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation and working process of the developed fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing strips for IMI detection. (A) Synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs, molecularly imprinted polymer solution, and SiO2 microspheres, and the assembly of the three-dimensional photonic crystal. (B) The adsorption-elution process of FMIHS strips.

Characterization of nitrogen-doped CDs. (A) TEM, inset: particle size distribution. (B) HRTEM, inset: single nitrogen-doped CDs. (C) Raman spectra. (D) FT-IR. (E) XPS and high-resolution XPS. (F) C 1 s, (G) N 1 s, and (H) O 1 s.

(A) Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs, inset: digital image of nitrogen-doped CDs under natural light and 365 nm UV. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra at different Ex wavelengths. (C) Fluorescence excitation-emission matrix for nitrogen-doped CDs. (D) Calibration curves for the fluorescence integral area and UV absorption of nitrogen-doped CDs and QS. (E) Fluorescence stability and photobleaching resistance of nitrogen-doped CDs. (F) Salt stability of nitrogen-doped CDs.

(A) SiO2 nanoparticles, inset: particle size distribution. (B) SEM images of self-assembled PC film, (C) FMIHS strips, and (D) FNIHS strips. (E) XPS spectra of FMIHS and FNIHS strips. (F) C 1 s spectra and (G) O 1 s spectra of FMIHS strips. (H) FT-IR spectra of FMIHS strips.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of FMIHS strips at various concentrations of IMI. (B) Standard curve depicting the fluorescence response of FMIHS strips in IMI solution at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 50 μg/mL. (C) Adsorption kinetics curves of FMIHS and FNIHS strips over different times. (D) Fluorescence response of FMIHS strips to the single analyte (50 μg/mL) and the mixed system (IMI: 50 μg/mL, interferents: 500 μg/mL). (E) Repeatability and (F) reproducibility of FMIHS strips in detecting IMI at 50 μg/mL.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime of nitrogen-doped CDs. (B) Plot of (Ahν)1/2 versus (hν) for E g, inset: UV–vis DRS of nitrogen-doped CDs. (C) FT-IR transmission spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs, IMI, and mixed system.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study integrated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (nitrogen-doped CDs) with remarkable fluorescence into a high-porosity inverse opal photonic crystal (IOPC) structure. A portable fluorescent hydrogel strip was developed by incorporating molecular imprinted biomimetic recognition, enabling the rapid identification and accurate detection of the insecticide imidacloprid (IMI). The ordered and hierarchical architecture of the IOPCs was advantageous to the uniform dispersion of nitrogen-doped CDs while providing efficient mass transfer channels for IMI. Additionally, the sensing strips achieved adsorption equilibrium within 20 min and demonstrated excellent selectivity, stability, and reusability. They showed a linear response to IMI across the range of 0.1–50 μg/mL (R 2 = 0.9905) with a detection limit of 0.065 μg/mL (S/N = 3). The spiked recoveries ranged from 88.2 % to 102.8 %, aligning well with HPLC results. This indicates that the developed fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing strip is an effective analytical tool for detecting IMI residues in food products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Imidacloprid (IMI), a broad-spectrum and highly effective neonicotinoid insecticide, exerts its effects by selectively binding to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors in the insect nervous system (Wang et al., 2023). This inhibition disrupts normal neural transmission within the central nervous system, leading to paralysis and death, thereby protecting crops and food products from pest infestations (Goulson, 2013). However, excessive use of IMI may lead to residue levels in fruits and vegetables exceeding the permissible limits, posing a risk of multi-organ toxicity, including damage to the liver, kidneys, and brain, and in severe cases, potentially leading to death (Pang et al., 2020). To mitigate this risk, China's National Food Safety Standard (GB 2763., 2021) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have stipulated the maximum residue limit (MRL) for IMI at 1 mg/kg in cucumbers and 0.5 mg/kg in apples. Additionally, the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) have established an Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) for IMI of 0.06 mg/kg body weight. This measure aims to mitigate potential health risks associated with pesticide residues in the human daily diet ( Joint meeting of the FAO panel of experts on pesticides residues in food and the environment and the WHO core assessment group on pesticide residues (2001: geneva, switzerland), FAO panel of experts on pesticide residues in food and the environment, & WHO core assessment group on pesticide residues. (2001). pesticide residues in food: 2001 / report of the joint meeting of the FAO panel of experts on pesticides residues in food and the environment and the WHO core assessment group on pesticide residues, Geneva, Switzerland, 2001). To ensure food safety, a variety of analytical techniques have been developed for the detection of IMI residues, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Ma et al., 2023; Voigt, Langerbein and Jaeger, 2022; Zareiyan, Khajehsharifi, & Rajabi, 2024), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Mozzaquatro, César, Pinheiro, & Caldas, 2022; Sánchez-Hernández et al., 2014). Although these methods offer high accuracy and reproducibility, their drawbacks, such as high cost, time consumption, and the need for complex sample preparation, restrict their practical applicability. Therefore, it is essential to establish a portable, rapid, sensitive, and efficient quantitative analytical method for detecting IMI residues in food. In previous studies, Zhai et al. (2023) developed colorimetric enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Co-ELISA) and chemiluminescent enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Cl-ELISA) methods for analyzing IMI residues in cabbage, cucumber, and zucchini. The Co-ELISA exhibited a linear range from 1.56 μg/L to 200 μg/L, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.56 μg/L, while the Cl-ELISA demonstrated a linear range from 0.19 μg/L to 25 μg/L, with an LOD of 0.19 μg/L. The study of Luo et al. (2022) fabricated an electrode modified using graphene oxide/gold nano/β-cyclodextrin films, showing a strong linear correlation between the reduction current response and IMI concentrations from 5 × 10−10 to 3000 × 10−10 mol/L. Nevertheless, these approaches were costly and required complex electrode modification procedures, exhibiting notable limitations in terms of environmental adaptability.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are rigid materials formed through the co-polymerization of template molecules and functional monomers under the influence of a crosslinking agent. Subsequently, the template molecules are removed, leaving specific recognition cavities (Pan et al., 2024). The cavities and recognition sites formed in the molecular imprinting system complement the structural and functional groups of the template molecule. This facilitates specific and selective recognition of the target compound. In addition, owing to their excellent accessibility and functional versatility, MIPs have garnered widespread attention in biomimetic recognition, simulated catalysis, and various other fields (Pan, Chen, Ma, & Pan, 2018; Vasapollo et al., 2011; Zhang, 2020; Zhang, Zhang, Jamal, & Abdiryim, 2024). Molecularly imprinted hydrogels incorporated imprinting sites within the hydrogel matrix. These imprinting sites exhibit remarkable compatibility with target molecules' shape, size, or functional groups. The integration of imprinting sites into the three-dimensional network of the hydrogel not only combines the inherent biocompatibility and rapid swelling response of the hydrogel but also endows the ability to selectively recognize and adsorb target molecules. These attributes collectively enhance efficient mass transfer, precise recognition, and controlled release of target molecules (Pan et al., 2024). Fluorescent molecular imprinting sensors, as a novel biomimetic sensing system, have gained significant attention in recent years. By combining the exceptional sensitivity of fluorescence detection with the outstanding selectivity of molecular imprinting technology, they utilize fluorescence enhancement or quenching as the signal transduction mechanism. This enables efficient and highly specific detection of target molecules or compounds.

Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have attracted widespread attention in various research fields due to their good water solubility, low toxicity, surface modifiability, and biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2018). In particular, due to their high quantum yield, excellent photostability, and resistance to photobleaching, they have demonstrated enormous application potential in fluorescence analysis and detection. Usually, researchers believe that nitrogen doping can promote the n-π* electrons transition, thereby effectively improving the fluorescence performance of CDs and significantly enhancing their fluorescence intensity (Bu et al., 2019). Additionally, the surface of nitrogen-doped CDs abounds in hydrophilic nitrogen functional groups, which not only improve water solubility but also enhance their specific binding with target molecules, further boosting fluorescence response. Consequently, fluorescence analysis utilizing nitrogen-doped CDs demonstrated the advantages of high efficiency, rapid response, and high sensitivity, making them widely applicable in the fields of sensor development (Li et al., 2023; Wang, Li, Li, & Dong, 2020), bioimaging (Atchudan et al., 2018; Zulfajri et al., 2020), environmental monitoring (Xing, Wang, & Wang, 2024), drug delivery (Kaurav, Verma, Bansal, Kapoor, & Sheth, 2023), and food safety (Shi, Zhao, & Liang, 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Inverse opal photonic crystals (IOPCs) are materials distinguished by a three-dimensional structure with large pores and multiple mass transfer channels. These structures were fabricated using porous SiO2 or other opals as templates, which were subsequently infiltrated with different materials or precursor solutions. After polymerization and curing, the porous templates were removed, resulting in the formation of the IOPCs (Fookes, Parada and Fidalgo, 2023). IOPCs possess abundant pores and a large specific surface area. Their stable, spatially periodic structure facilitates the formation of independent spectral bands, and the slow photon effect also amplifies the fluorescence signal of CDs, providing not only a wide range of mass transfer channels but also creating conditions for the uniform dispersion and enhanced signal response of CDs. Furthermore, IOPCs integrated with molecularly imprinted hydrogels have been shown to amplify the fluorescence response of the molecularly imprinted system, thus improving the sensitivity of detection systems (Xie et al., 2022). Consequently, the combination of fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs and IOPCs could significantly contribute to the development of high-performance fluorescent molecular imprinted sensing systems.

In this study, fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs were successfully integrated into a molecularly imprinted biomimetic recognition system based on an inverse SiO2 opal structure, leading to the development of fluorescence molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing (FMIHS) strips for efficient detection of pesticide IMI residue. The superior fluorescence properties of the synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs, combined with the well-ordered and hierarchical structure of the IOPCs, enabled the hydrogel strips to exhibit a rapid, highly sensitive fluorescence response. The developed fluorescent sensing strips can selectively identify IMI residues while demonstrating stability and reusability, thereby offering a novel strategy for accurately monitoring IMI contamination.

All reagents and solvents utilized in this study were of analytical reagent grade and employed without further purification. The target analyte IMI (99 %) was purchased from Heowns OPDE Technologies, LLC (Tianjin, China). Structural and functional analogs, including thiamethoxam (TMX, 99.5 %), acetamiprid (ACE, 97 %), and carbaryl (CBL, 98 %), were obtained from Solomen Biotech Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Methyl parathion (MP, 98.7 %) was sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamins A, B6, C, and E were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid and urea used to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate (QS), serving as the reference material for determining the quantum yield of nitrogen-doped CDs, along with the functional monomer methacrylic acid (MAA) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), were sourced from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The cross-linker ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and the polymerization initiator azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from TCI Shanghai Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NH3·H2O (25 %), NaCl, CH3COOH, HF (40 %), and other organic reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was generated using a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, USA). Standard glass slides alongside polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) sheets (38.0 × 12.5 × 1 mm), used for fabricating fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing (FMIHS) and fluorescent non-molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing (FNIHS) strips were supplied by Yishang Rubber Products Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

An electric heating air-blast drying oven (WHLL-125BE, TAISITE, China) was used for synthesizing nitrogen-doped CDs and IOPCs. Thermal polymerization processes for preparing FMIHS and FNIHS strips were carried out using a water bath (SY-1-2, Honour, China). UV absorbance data were recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence intensity of nitrogen-doped CDs and FMIHS strips was analyzed by using a fluorescence spectrometer (LUMINA, Thermo, USA). The size and morphological characteristics of the materials were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Apreo, Thermo, USA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). Functional group analysis of the material surfaces was performed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, IS50, Nicolet, USA), while the elemental composition and molecular structure were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped CDs, porous SiO2, and three-dimensional photonic crystal films
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1 mol of citric acid (0.5 g) and 20 mol of urea (2.5 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water. The resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The liquid product was cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged to remove the large particles. The filtration was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the collected products were dialyzed for 48 h (the molecular weight of the dialysis tape: 500 Da). The obtained nitrogen-doped CDs dispersion was stored at 4 °C and utilized for further experiments.

The SiO2 nanoparticles used in the study were synthesized via the modified traditional Stöber process, involving the hydrolysis of the silane coupling agent TEOS in an alkaline medium. The detailed procedure was as follows: in a chromic acid-treated round-bottomed flask, 15.4 mL of NH3·H2O (25 %) was mixed with 4.6 mL of deionized water, and the volume was adjusted to 100 mL with ethanol. Subsequently, another solution consisting of ethanol (50.0 mL) and TEOS (2.29 mL) was rapidly added to the mixture under vigorous stirring. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h, and the white solid product was collected via centrifugation. The solid was thoroughly washed with ethanol, dried at 50 °C for 12 h, and stored at room temperature for further applications. After soaking in a H2SO4/H2O2 mixture (7:3, v/v) for 24 h, the ordinary glass sheets (38.0 × 12.5 × 1.0 mm) were sequentially cleaned by ultrasonication in ethanol followed by deionized water, and then dried under nitrogen gas. The hydrophilic-treated glass sheets were subsequently vertically immersed into a SiO2 microsphere-ethanol suspension (1 %), allowing sedimentation self-assembly to occur at 50 °C, resulting in three-dimensional PC films characterized by an orderly arrangement of SiO2 microspheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel sensing strips
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 mmol of the template molecule IMI (25.56 mg) and 0.4 mmol of the functional monomer MAA (33.93 μL) were dissolved in 5 mL of acetonitrile. Subsequently, 200 μL of the nitrogen-doped CDs solution was added and mixed thoroughly. Furthermore, 604 μL of the cross-linker EGDMA (3.2 mmol) and 10 mg of AIBN as the initiator were introduced into the reaction mixture. After undergoing ultrasound treatment for 15 min, nitrogen gas was bubbled through the solution to remove dissolved oxygen, resulting in the formation of a molecularly imprinted precursor solution. The preparation of the non-imprinted precursor solution followed the same procedure as that for the imprinted precursor, but without the addition of the IMI template. A glass plate modified with three-dimensional PCs and a PMMA substrate of identical dimensions were fixed at both ends, allowing for the slow injection of either molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) or non-molecularly imprinted polymers (NIPs) precursor solutions into their interstitial space between the substrates to form a “sandwich” structure. This structure was then protected with nitrogen sealed, and thermally polymerized in a water bath at 60 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, it was immersed in a 5 % HF solution and soaked until it detached from the glass sheet; soaking was continued for an additional 12 h to etch SiO2 nanospheres from its surface. The obtained FMIHS strips were rinsed with ultrapure water and then washed with a methanol: acetic acid (9:1, v/v) solution to ensure complete removal of the IMI template molecules before being stored in ultrapure water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection procedure and real sample pretreatment
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared FMIHS strips were immersed within various concentrations (0.1, 1, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μg/mL) of IMI-methanol solutions (5.0 mL) and incubated at room temperature for 20 min. Subsequently, the fluorescent hydrogel strips were positioned at a 45° angle and immersed in sample cells containing the corresponding IMI-methanol solution. Fluorescence intensity was measured using a LUMINA fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength (Ex) of 360 nm, a scanning wavelength range of 440–550 nm, a detector voltage of 500 V, and a slit width of 10 nm.

The samples utilized in this study, which included apples, cucumbers, and cabbages, were sourced from a local supermarket in Tianjin. Five independent samples of each matrix were evaluated individually to ensure representativeness and reproducibility. All samples were pre-treated to eliminate the matrix interferences according to the GB/T 23379–2009 standard (GB/T 23379, 2009). For quality control during the testing process, the IMI standard working solutions were quantified using HPLC before use. Spectrophotometers used for fluorescence detection and HPLC equipment were calibrated regularly in accordance with specified requirements. Each sample underwent independent testing a minimum of three times to ensure accuracy and reliability. The obtained data were analyzed through comparison using mean and standard deviation. Specifically, the detection results from HPLC and FMIHS strips for IMI targets in samples were fitted, recoveries were calculated based on the formula given in Eq. S1, and the differences between the two methods were evaluated using a t-test (p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and working process of FMIHS strips for IMI detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 demonstrated the working principle of FMIHS strips in detecting IMI. Nitrogen-doped CDs exhibiting superior fluorescence characteristics were fabricated via a hydrothermal synthesis, while uniformly sized SiO2 microspheres were prepared using the Stöber method. Three-dimensional PCs were fabricated through self-assembly by immersing hydrophilic-treated glass slides into a suspension of SiO2 microspheres in ethanol. Subsequently, a molecularly imprinted polymer solution containing nitrogen-doped CDs, IMI, the functional monomer MAA, the crosslinker EGDMA, and the initiator AIBN was injected between the PMMA and glass slides, followed by thermal polymerization. After removing the SiO2 template, IOPCs with an ordered macroporous structure were obtained. Finally, the template molecule IMI was eluted, creating specific recognition sites and completing the preparation of FMIHS strips. The macroporous structure of the FMIHS strips demonstrated high specificity for recognizing and adsorbing IMI. This led to the quenching of fluorescence signals from the nitrogen-doped CDs, enabling FMIHS strips to achieve fast and sensitive recognition and quantitative analysis of IMI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of nitrogen-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the prepared nitrogen-doped CDs was analyzed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2A, the nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited nearly spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 1.54 nm, consistent with the morphology of quantum dots and demonstrating good dispersibility without aggregation. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2B) revealed that the nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the lattice spacing of graphene (100) (Yang et al., 2021). This observation indicated the presence of a graphitic carbon core structure, consistent with the formation of nitrogen-doped CDs with a well-ordered lattice. Typically, the nitrogen-doped CDs consist of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon nuclei and surface functional groups. The Raman spectra in Fig. 2C indicated that the prepared nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited two characteristic peaks at 1353 and 1591 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. These peaks were attributed to disordered sp3 carbon and ordered sp2 carbon, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.89. These results further confirm the graphitization of the prepared nitrogen-doped CDs.

The elemental composition and surface chemical groups of the nitrogen-doped CDs were investigated by FT-IR and XPS. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2D), the broad and distinct absorption peak at 3425 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The absorption band at 1646 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibrations of C=O/C=N. The absorption bands at 1388 cm−1 and 1032 cm−1 corresponded to the CH or CN bending and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. As depicted in the XPS spectra (Fig. 2E), typical spectral lines for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s were observed at 285.07, 399.53, and 531.37 eV, with proportions of 59.94 %, 12.79 %, and 27.27 %, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the spectral lines of C 1 s at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.3 eV were attributed to the C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O, and C=O/C=N groups, respectively ( Fig. 2F). The spectral lines at 399.9 eV and 401.2 eV in the N 1 s spectrum signified the presence of amino N and pyridine N (Fig. 2G). The O 1 s peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.6 eV corresponded to CO and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 2H). These results demonstrated that the surface of the nitrogen-doped CDs is rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which provided more reactive sites and enhanced the specificity and fluorescence properties.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 3A) of the prepared nitrogen-doped CDs revealed a weak absorption peak in the range of 230–350 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds within the sp2 conjugated system (Lai et al., 2020). The broad and intense absorption peak observed in the 300–450 nm range was assigned to the n-π* transition of the C=O/C-N bonds on the surface of the nitrogen-doped CDs, suggesting that the fluorescence emission of the nitrogen-doped CDs might be closely associated with surface defects. In addition, the gap between the maximum excitation wavelength (Ex) and the ultraviolet absorption wavelength was attributed to the non-radiative relaxation of the high-energy nitrogen-doped CDs. The inset images illustrated the appearance of the nitrogen-doped CDs dispersions under natural light and upon excitation with 365 nm light. In daylight, the dispersions appeared as transparent pale yellow, while emitted bright blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm light. Fig. 3B illustrated the fluorescence emission spectra of the nitrogen-doped CDs at various Ex. As Ex varied from 320 nm to 390 nm, the emission wavelength (Em) remained stable at approximately 450 nm, indicating that Em was independent of Ex. The highest fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen-doped CDs was observed at an Ex of 360 nm, consistent with the results obtained from the excitation-emission matrix in Fig. 3C. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen-doped CDs failed to display a consistent trend across different excitation wavelengths, primarily due to the variation in excitation efficiency of the nitrogen-doped CDs fluorophores at different Ex wavelengths. Specifically, the excitation wavelength must align with the specific absorption spectrum of the nitrogen-doped CDs for effective excitation. Additionally, different Ex wavelengths could induce different energy level transitions of fluorophores, ultimately influencing the fluorescence intensity. Further investigation into the fluorescence quantum yield revealed a value as high as 29.3 %, relative to quinine sulfate (quantum yield: 54 %) used as a reference standard (Fig. 3D), indicating its commendable fluorescent properties. Notably, the intense blue fluorescence emission was likely attributed to molecular fluorophores that were prone to photobleaching. Therefore, further studies on the photostability and resistance of the nitrogen-doped CDs were conducted (Fig. 3E). Under light-avoidance conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited no significant variation over a storage period of 0 to 14 days, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of only 3.2 % (n = 8). After 14 days of exposure to natural light, their fluorescence intensity retained approximately 93 % of its original intensity. Considering the complexity of food matrices, the salt resistance of the nitrogen-doped CDs was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 3F, the fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen-doped CDs remained stable even at NaCl concentrations as high as 10 mM, with an RSD value of only 4.4 % across six measurements. These results confirmed that the synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs still exhibit excellent fluorescence stability in complex environments, while maintaining high responsiveness and sensitivity, thereby laying a solid foundation for the development of stable and reliable fluorescent sensing devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SiO2 nanoparticles, three-dimensional PC films, and FMIHS strips
ce:section.ce:para: Under ammonia catalysis, TEOS underwent hydrolysis and subsequent polymerization, forming smooth-surfaced and uniformly dispersed SiO2 nanospheres with an average size of 200 nm (Fig. 4A). These nanospheres were used to fabricate three-dimensional PC films, which exhibited a highly ordered structure characterized by a typical face-centered cubic arrangement. However, the films exhibited structural defects, including cracks, voids, and dislocations (Fig. 4B). These defects were primarily attributed to the shrinkage and evaporation of SiO2 nanospheres during the self-assembly process of the three-dimensional PC film. The resulting stress between the microspheres and the glass substrate ultimately led to the formation of cracks.

Fig. 4C and D showed the SEM images of the prepared FMIHS and FNIHS strips, respectively, and there were significant morphological differences between the two. This is primarily because etching to remove the SiO2 nanospheres results in a highly ordered, inter-crosslinked three-dimensional macroporous structure on the surface of the FMIHS strip, which facilitates the formation and uniform distribution of specific recognition sites for IMI. This structure enables rapid mass transfer and targeted binding. In contrast, the non-imprinted hydrogel strip, synthesized without IMI, exhibited a disordered and fragmented structure, which hindered the formation of specific recognition sites. In Fig. 4E, the XPS spectra of the FMIHS and FNIHS strips were almost identical, indicating the complete elution of IMI from the FMIHS strip surface. In addition, the peaks observed at 284.73, 403.43, and 530.27 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively, with relative intensities of 71.94 % for C 1 s, 1.19 % for N 1 s, and 26.87 % for O 1 s. In the high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 4F), the peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.6 eV were attributed to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O, and CO groups, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4G) showed peaks at 531.8 and 533.3 eV, corresponding to the CO and CO functional groups, respectively. The N 1 s spectra exhibited peaks at 399.0 eV and 401.5 eV, which were attributed to amino N and pyridine N structures, respectively. Due to the low N content (1.19 %) in the imprinting system, no noticeable peaks were observed in the N 1 s region of the XPS spectra (Fig. S1). The FT-IR spectra of the FMIHS and FNIHS strips were presented in Fig. 4H. The absorption peaks observed at 2999 cm−1 and 2941 cm−1 were assigned to CH stretching vibrations, while the band at 1720 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 1426 cm−1 and 1379 cm−1 corresponded to CN stretching vibrations and CH bending, respectively. Additionally, the bands at 1241 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 were attributed to C-O-C stretching vibrations. These results provide strong evidence for the successful preparation of FMIHS strips.

In this study, the nitrogen-doped CDs acted as fluorescent signal sources for FMIHS strips, with their dosage directly affecting the fluorescence response. Consequently, a detailed investigation was carried out to optimize their dosage. As shown in Fig. S2, the imprinting factor (IF) exhibited a trend of increasing initially and then decreasing, with the IF of FMIHS strips consistently higher than that of FNIHS strips. The maximum IF (5.9) was achieved at a dosage of 200 μL of nitrogen-doped CDs. However, further increasing the dosage of nitrogen-doped CDs resulted in a significant decrease in the recognition ability of FMIHS strips towards the IMI target, consequently reducing detection sensitivity. This phenomenon was attributed to the interaction between the excited state energy levels of nitrogen-doped CDs within the FMIHS strip system. Such interaction interfered with electronic transitions between neighboring nitrogen-doped CDs. When electrons returned to the ground state from the excited state, energy was dissipated as non-fluorescent emissions, such as heat. This reduced the overall fluorescence intensity of the strip and prevented the nitrogen-doped CDs from functioning optimally. Additionally, an excessive concentration of nitrogen-doped CDs might prevent the template molecule from binding to the functional monomer and interfere with the re-binding of the target molecule to the recognition sites. In the preparation of FMIHS strips, the molar ratio of the template molecule (IMI) to the functional monomer (MAA) was optimized to enhance the specific recognition capability of the hydrogel strips. The IF value peaked at 5.5 at a molar ratio of 1:4 for IMI to MAA (Fig. S3). To preserve the integrity and stability of the three-dimensional macroporous structure of FMIHS strips, cross-linking agents were introduced to establish a rigid skeleton, thereby promoting the formation of nanopore cavities and specific binding sites for the template molecules. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the optimal specific binding sites, yielding an IF value of 5.8, were achieved when the cross-linker EGDMA was used at 15 times the amount of the functional monomer. Excessive cross-linking agents, however, diminished the “flexibility” of the binding sites in the hydrogels, thereby hindering rapid mass transfer and adsorption of target molecules (Cao, Liu, Qin, Li, & Liu, 2021). Under optimal conditions, FMIHS strips exhibited a stable yet flexible three-dimensional structure with a high specific surface area. The structure provided abundant binding sites, facilitating rapid and selective recognition and elution of template molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response performance of the FMIHS strip
ce:section.ce:para:
Upon incorporating IMI molecules into the fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel structure, they were specifically captured, resulting in the release of excited state energy from nitrogen-doped CDs via a non-radiative transition. This led to fluorescence quenching of the hydrogel strip. This fluorescence response (F 0/F) correlated significantly with the concentration of quenched molecules, facilitating the quantitative determination of IMI. Fig. 5A illustrated the fluorescence response of the FMIHS strip at varying concentrations of IMI. As IMI concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of FMIHS strips gradually decreased. Notably, clear fluorescence intensity was detectable even at a low concentration of 0.1 μg/mL. Fig. 5B presented the relationship between fluorescence response (F 0/F) and IMI concentrations for FMIHS strips, which followed the Stern-Volmer equation (Pan, Wang, et al., 2024).
F 0
/ F =
K sv
C IMI
+ 1 .
where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of IMI, respectively. K SV denotes the quenching constant, and C IMI denotes the IMI concentrations. Over the range from 0.1 to 50 μg/mL, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence response of FMIHS strips and IMI concentration was observed (R 2 = 0.9905). The detection limit (LOD), defined as the concentration corresponding to a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, was calculated to be 0.065 μg/mL. These findings indicated that the FMIHS strip displayed high sensitivity and accuracy in the detection of IMI. However, as IMI concentration increased further, the linear correlation between IMI concentration and fluorescence response was compromised. This phenomenon was likely attributed to the saturation effect of IMI at high concentrations on the fluorescence detection system.

Fig. 5C illustrated the results of the adsorption kinetics analysis for the developed FMIHS strip on the target IMI. At an equivalent adsorption duration, the fluorescence quenching response of the FMIHS strip in a 50 μg/mL IMI solution was significantly higher than that of the FNIHS strip. This enhanced response was attributed to the presence of three-dimensional cavities and well-defined recognition sites on the FMIHS strip, which were complementary to the IMI molecule. After 20 min of exposure to an IMI standard solution, the F 0/F value reached its maximum and stabilized, indicating the rapid mass transfer rate for the developed FMIHS strip. Subsequently, four insecticides (TMX, ACE, CBL, MP) structurally or functionally similar to IMI, were selected as controls to evaluate the specificity of the developed sensing strips. As illustrated in Fig. 5D, the FMIHS strip exhibited a significant fluorescence quenching response to IMI, with minimal response to other target molecules. This specificity could be attributed to the synergistic effect between the specific recognition sites within the MIP and high mass transfer channels in its inverse opal structure, enhancing the selectivity of FMIHS strips for detecting IMI. Furthermore, potential interferents, including TMX, ACE, CEL, and MP (500 μg/mL), as well as typical natural interferents such as vitamins A, B6, C, and E (500 μg/mL), were introduced to evaluate the anti-interference capabilities of the developed FMIHS strip. In both binary and five-component mixed systems, the FMIHS strip exhibited a stable fluorescence response. This indicated that, even in the presence of high concentrations of multiple interferents, the developed FMIHS strip could effectively recognize the target IMI. However, under the same conditions, the FMIHS strip without template molecule preparation consistently exhibited a lower fluorescence response in the mixed systems.

The fluorescence response of the prepared FMIHS strip after five cycles of adsorption-elution was demonstrated in Fig. 5E, demonstrating a distinct attenuation trend. The RSD of the fluorescence response measured over five consecutive cycles was only 1.3 % (n = 5), indicating the reversible recognition capability for the target IMI and confirming its reusability. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5F, the reproducibility of both the preparation and operation processes was validated by recording an RSD of 1.7 % (n = 5) for the fluorescence responses obtained from five batches of FMIHS strips exposed to a consistent concentration of IMI standard solution (50 μg/mL). Fig. S5 illustrated the change in fluorescence intensity of the prepared FMIHS strip during continuous storage for 14 days with an RSD value of 1.2 %. Additionally, Fig. S6 and S7 depicted the fluorescence intensity variations of the FMIHS strip under varying conditions, including different ionic strengths and pH values, with corresponding RSD values of 2.6 % and 1.1 %, respectively. These results demonstrate that the developed FMIHS strips exhibit excellent adaptability to complex external environments and notable storage stability.

As reported by Hu et al. (2022), the primary fluorescence quenching mechanisms included static quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE). As illustrated in Fig. S8, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited minimal changes before and after the addition of IMI, indicating that no new compounds were formed, thus ruling out the possibility of static quenching. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of IMI and the fluorescence emission spectrum of nitrogen-doped CDs showed minimal overlap (Fig. S9), excluding FRET and IFE as potential quenching mechanisms. Consequently, PET was likely responsible for the fluorescence quenching of nitrogen-doped CDs by IMI. To further elucidate the quenching mechanism of the fluorescent source nitrogen-doped CDs in FMIHS strips, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay measurements were performed on the solution, and the data were fitted using the following Eqs. (1) and (2) (Yan et al., 2023) (Fig. 6A).

Among them,

represent the fluorescence decay lifetimes, while A1 and A2 are their respective exponential pre-factors. According to Table S1,

accounted for a dominant proportion (98.4 %), indicating that the fluorescence primarily originated from surface states. Subsequently, the structural model of nitrogen-doped CDs was simulated computationally, and the energy gap was calculated using first-principles density-functional theory (DFT). Fig. S10 showed the optimized geometric model of nitrogen-doped CDs, including the electron distributions in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The energy gap of the nitrogen-doped CDs was calculated to be 2.96 eV, which was consistent with the surface state emission characteristics reported for the citric acid system in previous studies (Guo et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2020). Notably, the obtained value closely approximated the bandgap energy (E g = 2.92 eV) derived from fitting the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs using the Tauc plot (Fig. 6B), further corroborating that nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited surface state luminescence characteristics. Based on these findings, it was hypothesized that the fluorescence quenching of nitrogen-doped CDs by IMI might be attributed to the highly reactive groups present on their surface. To validate this hypothesis, the FT-IR spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs, IMI, and their mixed system were compared (Fig. 6C). The prominent absorption at 1580 cm−1 for IMI was attributed to the stretching vibration of the -NO2 group, while the peak at 1290 cm−1 corresponded to CN stretching vibrations. These spectral features indicated the presence of electron-accepting groups such as -NO2 and heterocyclic nitrogen within the IMI molecules (Fig. S11). Therefore, surface functional groups of nitrogen-doped CDs, such as -COOH and -NH2, could transfer charge to the electron-accepting groups of IMI, thereby modifying the electronic structure at the surface. According to the FT-IR analysis of the mixed system of nitrogen-doped CDs and IMI, a significant enhancement in the absorption intensity of the OH stretching vibration at 3430 cm−1 and the CH stretching vibration of the aliphatic chain at 2925 cm−1. This further confirmed that IMI molecules induced alterations in the luminescence center of nitrogen-doped CDs through charge transfer, ultimately leading to fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of IMI in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of the developed FMIHS strips for detecting IMI in real samples, commercially available apple, cabbage, and cucumber samples were selected and spiked with IMI at three concentration levels (10, 30, and 50 μg/g). After a simple pre-treatment process, these samples were analyzed using the developed FMIHS strip, enabling the assessment of their effectiveness in complex food matrices. Table 1 presented the recoveries for the three selected samples across all spiked concentrations, ranging from 88.2 % to 102.8 %, with an RSD of less than 4.9 % (n = 3). The t-test (p < 0.05) results indicated that there was no significant difference between the results obtained from the sensing strip and those derived from the standard HPLC method, with a correlation coefficient exceeding 0.98 (Fig. S12). Consequently, the FMIHS strip developed in this study provided an accurate, stable, and effective analytical strategy for detecting IMI targets in fruit and vegetable matrices.

Additionally, Table 2 outlined the characteristics of various IMI detection strategies reported in the literature. In comparison, the detection limit and linear range of the FMIHS strip-based fluorescence analysis strategy were superior to those reported in the literature. This demonstrates that the proposed FMIHS strips, based on nitrogen-doped CDs and IOPCs, facilitate rapid, accurate, and sensitive on-site detection of IMI residues in food.

In this work, a highly effective FMIHS strip with specific recognition capabilities for IMI was successfully developed, utilizing fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs and biomimetic molecular imprinting recognition. Furthermore, the incorporation of a photonic crystal with an inverse opal structure facilitated the uniform and efficient anchoring of nitrogen-doped CDs, establishing a pathway for rapid mass transfer and recognition of IMI. These portable FMIHS strips demonstrate rapid and sensitive fluorescence responses, along with precise recognition of IMI, enabling convenient quantitative analysis of IMI pesticides in real fruit and vegetable samples. This study presents a strategy for constructing user-friendly biomimetic testing strips. The approach can be extended to facilitate rapid and accurate quantitative analyses of other targets in various fields.
